items.title: 2025 Canada's Top Ten Shorts Programme 01
items.imgAlt: Weathered vintage puppet or doll with painted face in dark, shadowy setting
items.description: Post-screening Q&A with Lesley Loksi Chan (Lloyd Wong, Unfinished) and Zion Saffer (Jazz Infernal).

items.title: 2025 Canada's Top Ten Shorts Programme 02
items.imgAlt: Silhouette of a person standing alone at night against a dark blue sky with shadowy trees below
items.description: Post-screening Q&A with Gavin Baird (Klee), Martin Edralin (La Mayordomía), Shervin Kermani (Ramón Who Speaks to Ghosts), and Nic Altobelli (ripe).

items.title: A Poet
items.imgAlt: A person in glasses and a red striped shirt laughing heartily while in motion
items.description: In A Poet, an aging, obscure writer mentors a talented teen, hoping to give her what he never had. But their bond — rooted in good intentions — spirals into a web of poor decisions and the weight of unmet ambitions.

items.title: A Private Life
items.imgAlt: Woman in brown coat searching through vintage card catalog drawers in dimly lit library
items.description: Oscar winner Jodie Foster stars in this slyly comic psychological thriller from director Rebecca Zlotowski (TIFF ’22’s Other People's Children), in which a suspicious death yields a series of twists that lead back to old grievances — and maybe even to past lives.

items.title: A Tribe Called Love with Mohamed Ahmed and Say Somaali
items.imgAlt: Side profile of two people in an intimate embrace, backlit with warm lighting in a dark setting

guests.bio: Mohamed Ahmed grew up in Ottawa, Canada, before moving to Vancouver to pursue his passion for filmmaking at the Vancouver Film School (VFS). After graduating, he relocated to Toronto to immerse himself in the independent content scene. With years of experience working on film and television sets, Ahmed went on to create Sheeko Sheeko, a Somali web-based talk show that gained widespread recognition and was even featured on Vice’s Huang’s World. His short documentary My Home is Football, about a homeless soccer team, earned him an award, showcasing his talent for heartfelt storytelling. In addition, Ahmed created the web series Ayan’s World, a vibrant portrayal of Somali culture. Ahmed has recently completed his debut feature A Tribe Called Love.

items.title: A Wanderer's Notebook
webProgrammes: Daughters, Wives, and Mothers: The Films of Mikio Naruse

items.title: Agatha's Almanac with Amalie Atkins
items.imgAlt: Elderly person in striped pink dress standing beside weathered wall and tree trunk in dappled sunlight
items.description: Post-screening Q&A with Amalie Atkins (director).

guests.bio:
Amalie Atkins (she/her) is a multidisciplinary artist based in Saskatoon, Saskatchewan, Canada. Renowned for her films and video installations, Atkins creates cinematic fables by blending 16mm film, performance, textiles, installations, and analogue photography. Her practice merges traditional elements with a hands-on, do-it-yourself aesthetic to imprint a fictional world onto everyday life.

Her work has been featured in major survey exhibitions, including Oh, Canada at MASS MoCA, DreamLand: Textiles in the Canadian Landscape at the Textile Museum of Canada, and Road Show East, which toured Eastern Europe.

Her films have screened nationally and internationally, including at the Berlin International Art Film Festival, Bucharest Film Awards, Festival International Signes de Nuit, Montreal Independent Film Festival, Dresdner Schmalfilmtage, and Analogue Resilience.

items.title: All That's Left of You
items.imgAlt: Father lifts young son to reach fruit on tree branch during sunny afternoon outdoors

items.title: Arco
items.imgAlt: Colorful anime-style illustration with two characters against vibrant rainbow swirls and dynamic light effects
items.description:
Presented in the English dub, with a voice cast that includes Mark Ruffalo, Will Ferrell, and America Ferrera.

Time travel gets a magical spin in Ugo Bienvenu’s animated adventure — produced by Natalie Portman (whose voice is also featured) — a charming paean to friendship between two children from different eras and a warning about our failure to preserve our environment.

items.title: Atropia
items.imgAlt: Person wearing green hooded cloak with pearl trim laughing expressively against dark background

items.title: Belle de Jour
items.imgAlt: Portrait of a woman with blonde hair looking over her shoulder in a dimly lit room

items.title: Blue Heron with Sophy Romvari
items.imgAlt: Young girl in blue floral swimsuit holding video camera outdoors, yellow balloon visible in foreground
items.description: Post-screening Q&A with Sophy Romvari (director, producer, screenwriter).

guests.imageAlt: Black and white portrait of a smiling woman with dark hair looking over her shoulder outdoors
guests.bio: Sophy Romvari is a Canadian Hungarian filmmaker based in Toronto. Her critically acclaimed short films have travelled the international festival circuit and earned her a reputation as a leading young talent. She has screened at TIFF, Hot Docs, Sheffield, and True/False, and has received a retrospective at the Museum of the Moving Image. She has also directed films for CBC Short Docs and Kino Lorber. In 2020, Sophy completed her Master’s at York University. Her thesis film Still Processing premiered at TIFF in September 2020, was nominated for a Canadian Screen Award, and was featured in a collection of Sophy’s eight short films on the Criterion Channel. Her latest short film, It’s What Each Person Needs is available on The New Yorker and MUBI. Blue Heron is her feature debut.

items.title: Bugonia
items.imgAlt: Person with shaved head in floral dress gazes to the side in a warmly lit interior setting

items.title: Catherine Russell on Repast
items.imgAlt: Vintage black and white photo of Japanese people dining together at a restaurant table, sharing a meal
items.description:
Extended introduction by Distinguished University Professor of Film Studies at Concordia University, Catherine Russell. Repast screens again on December 16.

Saturday, November 29: Ticketholders are invited to join us in the cinema lobby from 2:30–4pm for a special treat! We’re marking the opening weekend of Daughters, Wives, and Mothers with a curated hand-roll sample, courtesy of Hello Nori, the series’ official Hospitality Partner.
webProgrammes: Daughters, Wives, and Mothers: The Films of Mikio Naruse

guests.bio: Catherine Russell is Distinguished University Professor of Film Studies at Concordia University in Montreal. She is the author of seven books, including The Cinema of Barbara Stanwyck: Twenty-Six Short Essays on a Working Star (Illinois University Press, 2023), Archiveology: Walter Benjamin and Archival Film Practices (Duke, 2018), The Cinema of Naruse Mikio: Women and Japanese Modernity (Duke, 2008), and Experimental Ethnography: The Work of Film in the Age of Video (Duke, 1999). Her articles on documentary cinema, Hollywood, archive theory, Japanese cinema, and experimental film have appeared in numerous journals and anthologies.

items.title: Daughters, Wives and a Mother
webProgrammes: Daughters, Wives, and Mothers: The Films of Mikio Naruse

items.title: Dead Lover
items.imgAlt: Two performers in dramatic lighting - one bathed in green light, another covered in red blood-like makeup
items.description:
Presented in Stink-o-Vision! Scratch-and-sniff cards will be provided — and be warned, the smells are pungent. Also featuring a post-screening Q&A with director and star Grace Glowicki and cast members Ben Petrie, Leah Doz, and Lowen Morrow, moderated by Weird Alice.

A wily gravedigger (Grace Glowicki) falls for the one man who is attracted to her fetid funk (Ben Petrie), but when fate doth conspire, she takes drastic measures to preserve their love in this camp phantasmagoria.

items.title: Dennis Doros Carte Blanche: The Connection
items.description: Extended introduction by Milestone Films co-founder Dennis Doros.

guests.bio: In 1990, Dennis Doros co-founded Milestone Films with his wife Amy Heller. Working with film archives and labs around the world, they have restored and distributed movies outside the mainstream, including works by Charles Burnett, Sherman Alexie, Billy Woodberry, Kathleen Collins, Ayoka Chenzira, Margot Benacerraf, Bridgett M. Davis, and Nancy Savoca. Milestone’s motto is “We like to fuck with the canon.” Doros served two terms as the Association of Moving Image Archivists’ president and on the Board of the Co-ordinating Council of Audiovisual Archives Associations. In 2022, Dennis and Amy helped co-found Missing Movies (www.missingmovies.org), an organization dedicated to assisting filmmakers reclaim their lost rights and locate their original materials. In 2025, Doros returned to his first restoration after forty years, Erich von Stroheim’s Queen Kelly.

items.title: Fire Will Come with recorded intro by Oliver Laxe
items.description: Recorded introduction by filmmaker Oliver Laxe.

items.title: Floating Clouds
items.imgAlt: Dramatic black and white portrait of a young Asian man in a suit and tie with contemplative expression
webProgrammes: Daughters, Wives, and Mothers: The Films of Mikio Naruse

items.title: Flowing
webProgrammes: Daughters, Wives, and Mothers: The Films of Mikio Naruse

items.title: Follies with Eric K. Boulianne
items.imgAlt: Two people sitting at a dimly lit table, woman in checkered sweater and man in dark clothing with glasses
items.description: Post-screening Q&A with Eric K. Boulianne (director, cast, producer, co-screenwriter), Laurie Pominville (producer), and Alexandre Auger (co-screenwriter).

guests.imageAlt: Black and white portrait of a bearded man wearing glasses, looking thoughtfully to the side
guests.bio: Eric K. Boulianne is a Montreal-based filmmaker, screenwriter, and actor. His writing credits include the features Prank (16) and Viking (22), both of which played the Festival. He’s directed several short films, including Montreal Zombies (12) and the Festival Official Selection Faire un enfant (20). Folichonneries (25) is his feature directorial debut.

Laurie Pominville holds a Bachelor’s degree in Cultural and Media Production Strategy from Université du Québec à Montréal, specializing in film production. After graduating, she joined the National Film Board of Canada’s Filmmaker Assistance Program as an associate producer at the Documentary Studio, working on projects such as Afterwards (Clermont-Ferrand 2024) by Romane Garant Chartrand. In 2022, she joined La Coop Vidéo de Montréal as a coordinator and began producing fiction and documentary. Folichonneries (Locarno 2025, TIFF) directed by Eric K. Boulianne and co-written with Alexandre Auger marked her first feature project. Driven by curiosity, she collaborates closely with filmmakers to create sensitive, engaged, and human cinema.

Alexandre Auger has written the short films Gaspé Copper, Pas La Grosse Sophie, Une Bombe Au Cœur, and Landgraves. He also co-wrote the feature films Yo (Rotterdam Festival), Prank (Venice Critics’ Week, TIFF ’16, BAFICI), Les Barbares de la Malbaie, and Bungalow (Tallinn Black Nights Film Festival). His upcoming films as a screenwriter, Gagne Ton Ciel (TIFF ’25), directed by Mathieu Denis, and Folichonneries (Locarno Film Festival, TIFF ’25), directed by Eric K. Boulianne, will both be released in 2026.

Auger is currently developing several feature film projects and TV series, in addition to working on more personal projects that he wants to helm as director.

items.title: Frankenstein
items.imgAlt: A man in period clothing holds up a small metallic object on stage in dramatic blue lighting
items.description:
Screening in 35mm!

Oscar-winning director Guillermo del Toro’s visually sumptuous adaptation of Mary Shelley’s gothic masterpiece finds Oscar Isaac as the brilliant scientist whose unearthly creation, eerily and ingeniously conjured by Jacob Elordi, blurs the boundaries between life, death, and madness.

items.title: Hamnet
items.imgAlt: Two people in period clothing stand in a mossy forest slope, surrounded by lush green vegetation
items.description: Academy Award–winning director Chloé Zhao (Nomadland, TIFF ’20 People’s Choice Award) helms this lush and tender drama about William Shakespeare (Paul Mescal) and his family, as seen through the eyes of his thoughtful wife Agnes (a luminous Jessie Buckley).

items.title: I Am Cuba, with Cameron Bailey
items.description: Recorded introduction by Milestone Films co-founder Dennis Doros, plus introduction by TIFF CEO Cameron Bailey, who will also lead a post-screening audience discussion.

guests.bio: CEO, TIFF

items.title: International Cinema Cafe
items.imgAlt: International Cinema Cafe logo with red coffee cup icon and Lavazza partnership, Sundays at 10:30am
items.description: International Cinema Cafe provides a snapshot of the best cinema from around the world, with a focus on films submitted for Best International Feature Film at the Academy Awards and hidden gems that may not see wide release. Meet fellow film lovers and make new friends as you explore the best of international cinema on Sunday mornings with complimentary coffee and pastries.

guests.bio: Kelly Boutsalis is the International Programmer, Canadian Features, at the Toronto International Film Festival. Working at TIFF since 2022, she’s also contributed to Canada's Top Ten and co-hosted the International Cinema Cafe. She is Mohawk from the Six Nations of the Grand River reserve. As a freelance writer, she has written about film and television for The New York Times, NOW Magazine, ELLE Canada, Flare, Realscreen, and POV Magazine. The bulk of her journalism focuses on telling positive Indigenous stories. She has a BA in comparative literature from Western University, and studied journalism at Humber College.

Christoph Straub is the Director of Public Programming at TIFF, overseeing its talks and subscription series programming, TIFF Next Wave, Community Impact, Public Spaces and Seniors initiatives. Christoph is also the producer for In Conversation With…, which connects audiences with some of the most influential voices in film and television. Previously, he was the Manager of Industry Programming at TIFF, where he was responsible for its Talent Development initiatives and the Industry Conference at the Festival. He holds a master’s degree in American literature from Johannes Gutenberg University in Mainz, Germany.

items.title: KPop Demon Hunters
items.imgAlt: Three animated female warriors in stylish modern outfits pose with glowing weapons against a blue backdrop

items.title: L'Âge d'or preceded by Un chien andalou
items.description: Luis Buñuel and Salvador Dalí’s Un chien andalou and L'Âge d'or trace a Surrealist escalation from anarchic dream imagery to a full-blown rebellion against bourgeois society.

items.title: Late Chrysanthemums
webProgrammes: Daughters, Wives, and Mothers: The Films of Mikio Naruse

items.title: Laxe Carte Blanche: The Naked Island
items.description: Recorded introduction by filmmaker Oliver Laxe, discussing Kaneto Shindo’s The Naked Island in this carte blanche selection.

items.title: Learn to Swim with Thyrone Tommy
items.description: Introduction by TIFF lead programmer for Canada, Jason Anderson who will be joined by filmmaker Thyrone Tommy for a Q&A following the screening.

guests.bio: Thyrone Tommy is an award-winning filmmaker and alumnus of the Canadian Film Centre’s Directors’ Lab and TIFF Talent Lab. His debut feature, Learn to Swim, is a Telefilm Talent to Watch, CBC Films, and Toronto Arts Council–supported project that premiered at TIFF ’21 and was part of Canada’s Top Ten 2021. The film is distributed on Netflix in the US and worldwide through Ava DuVernay’s ARRAY Releasing, and in Canada through Mongrel Films. His films have been celebrated internationally at over 50 festivals, including his short Mariner, which premiered at TIFF ’16, won the Lindalee Tracey Award, and was part of Canada’s Top Ten 2016 before premiering online as a Vimeo Staff Pick. On the TV side, he has directed episodes of Revenge of the Best Black Friend (CBC) and Murdoch Mysteries (CBC).

items.title: Lightning with Kelley Dong
items.description: Introduction by writer, producer, and filmmaker Kelley Dong
webProgrammes: Daughters, Wives, and Mothers: The Films of Mikio Naruse

guests.bio: Kelley Dong is a writer, producer, and filmmaker. Their writing has appeared in MUBI Notebook, Cinema Scope, Film Comment, The Village Voice, and the Harvard Film Archive.

items.title: Lunar New Year 2026: Eve and the Fire Horse
items.imgAlt: Two young children lying on grass, wearing blue clothing, with matching dark hair and gentle smiles

guests.bio: Julia Kwan is a Vancouver-based filmmaker. Ms. Kwan’s dramatic feature film debut, Eve & the Fire Horse, won the Special Jury Prize at the Sundance Film Festival. Ms. Kwan has written and directed numerous films in different genres over the years, including the NFB’s Everything Will Be, her debut feature documentary and the NFB animation, The Zoo. Ms. Kwan teaches screenwriting and is currently completing her Masters in Creative Writing at the University of British Columbia.

Christine Vu is a Toronto-based actor and filmmaker, nominated for a 2023 Canadian Screen Award for their work on the 2022 Reel Asian Awards. They are currently developing the short Mabel’s Girl and finishing post-production on a doc about Science Is a Drag. Vu is also the Director of Creative Marketing and Communications at Reel Asian and a CFMDC board member.

items.title: Magellan
items.imgAlt: Bearded sailor in period costume aboard historic sailing ship at sunset, with rigging and red cross visible
items.description: Gael García Bernal stars as Ferdinand Magellan, in Lav Diaz’s grand, decolonial retelling of the Portuguese explorer’s life and expeditions.

items.title: Mile End Kicks
items.imgAlt: Young friends in sunglasses pose playfully with drinks outside an orange building with festive bunting overhead
items.description: I Like Movies director Chandler Levack’s Mile End Kicks is a bright romantic comedy starring Barbie Ferreira as Grace, a young music critic who moves to Montreal to figure out life and love.

items.title: Mimosas with recorded intro by Oliver Laxe

guests.bio: Catherine Russell is Distinguished University Professor of Film Studies at Concordia University in Montreal. She is the author of seven books, including The Cinema of Barbara Stanwyck: Twenty-Six Short Essays on a Working Star (Illinois University Press, 2023), Archiveology: Walter Benjamin and Archival Film Practices (Duke, 2018), The Cinema of Naruse Mikio: Women and Japanese Modernity (Duke, 2008), and Experimental Ethnography: The Work of Film in the Age of Video (Duke, 1999). Her articles on documentary cinema, Hollywood, archive theory, Japanese cinema, and experimental film have appeared in numerous journals and anthologies.

items.title: Mr. Nobody Against Putin
items.imgAlt: Side profile of a person in a dark room wearing glasses, with orange light reflecting off the lenses

items.title: Nirvanna the Band the Show the Movie with Jay McCarrol
items.imgAlt: Two men in casual suits walking on city street, one carrying orange extension cords while other gestures upward
items.description: Post-screening Q&A with Jay McCarrol (cast, composer of original score), Matt Greyson (producer), and Jared Raab (cinematographer, executive producer).

guests.bio: Jay McCarrol is a Canadian comedian, writer, musician, and performer best known as the co-creator and star of Nirvanna the Band the Show (2017–2019). He recently co-wrote, co-starred, and composed the next evolution of the franchise, Nirvanna the Band the Show the Movie (2026). McCarrol collaborated on BlackBerry (2023), for which he won a Canadian Screen Award for Best Original Score, and is composing Tony, a biopic about young Anthony Bourdain for A24, due out later this year. McCarrol also co-created the original animated series Matt & Bird Break Loose for Amazon, which he wrote, acted, composed, and animated. He has toured Canada as a pop star, written hit jingles, and is the former musical director of Second City.

Matt Greyson is a Canadian film producer whose work spans feature films and series. His long-standing collaborations with director Matt Johnson include BlackBerry, which premiered at the Berlin International Film Festival and went on to win a record number of Canadian Screen Awards. In 2025, Greyson produced Nirvanna the Band the Show the Movie, which had its world premiere at SXSW before its Canadian premiere in the Midnight Madness programme at TIFF, where it won the People’s Choice Award. Greyson has also produced series and digital projects including CORUS’ hit kid show Miss Persona and TVO’s New World Beat.

Jared Raab is an award-winning Canadian D.O.P. director, animator and editor. Originally coming from the world of music video and feature documentary, his long standing feature film collaborations with director Matt Johnson started with the Slamdance-winning film The Dirties in 2013. Their follow up, Operation Avalanche premiered at Sundance and was nominated for six CSA awards. Along with fellow members of Zapruder Films, their film BlackBerry, premiered in Berlin and garnered a record number of CSA wins including Best Cinematography. Last year, he was the director of Animation for the Peabody Award–winning documentary Any Other Way: The Jackie Shane Story.

items.title: No Other Choice
items.imgAlt: A family embrace joyfully in a garden setting with a brick wall and trees in the background
items.description: Adapted from a novel by Donald E. Westlake (The Ax), this incisive, darkly comic satire from Park Chan-wook (TIFF ’22’s Decision to Leave) follows a newly unemployed man who, desperate to land a coveted position, hatches a ruthless plan to dispatch his competition.

items.title: Our Land, Our Freedom with Meena Nanji and Zippy Kimundu
items.description: Introduction by Dr. Alison MacAulay, postdoctoral fellow at the University of Toronto Mississauga, who will be joined by filmmakers Meena Nanji and Zippy Kimundu for a Q&A following the screening.
webProgrammes: What Remains: Reclaiming Memories, Materialities, and Embodied Histories in African Cinema

guests.bio: Meena Nanji is Kenyan-born of Indian heritage. She is an award-winning director-producer of feature documentaries and experimental and fiction shorts. Her work has been recognized by the Sundance Documentary Fund, Just Films, Perspective Fund, IDA, and IDFA Bertha Fund. Her films have screened on television and at film festivals internationally and largely focus on women and social justice issues, as well as race and gender rights and representation, including projects filmed in India, Afghanistan, and Kenya. She is a co-founder of GlobalGirl Media, a non-profit organization that trains girls from underrepresented communities of the world in citizen journalism. She has also worked as a curator-programmer for film and video festivals, served as juror for foundation grants, and led film workshops and trainings, including in the USA, Morocco, South Africa, and India. She is based in Los Angeles.

Zippy Kimundu is an award-winning Kenyan filmmaker who has been working in the global industry for nearly two decades. She holds a Master’s in Fine Arts from New York University and has worked globally in over 20 countries. She co-directed the short documentary A Fork, A Spoon & A Knight with renowned director Mira Nair, and was the Assistant Editor on the Disney film Queen of Katwe. Her award-winning feature documentary Our Land, Our Freedom had its world premiere at IDFA/Sheffield in 2023, while her latest feature Widow Champion premiered at Hotdocs/Tribeca. She has made numerous shorts as well and continues to create powerful impact.

Zippy is the Creative Lead at The Terrace, a film educator at I’ll Tell You My Story, and founder of AfroFilms International, a creative collective working to ignite socio-political consciousness and action across continents.

items.title: Queen Kelly
items.description: Queen Kelly screens again on January 13 with an introduction and Q&A with Milestone Films co-founder Dennis Doros.

items.title: Queen Kelly with Dennis Doros
items.description: Introduction and Q&A with Milestone Films co-founder Dennis Doros. Queen Kelly screens again on January 24.

guests.bio: In 1990, Dennis Doros co-founded Milestone Films with his wife Amy Heller. Working with film archives and labs around the world, they have restored and distributed movies outside the mainstream, including works by Charles Burnett, Sherman Alexie, Billy Woodberry, Kathleen Collins, Ayoka Chenzira, Margot Benacerraf, Bridgett M. Davis, and Nancy Savoca. Milestone’s motto is “We like to fuck with the canon.” Doros served two terms as the Association of Moving Image Archivists’ president and on the Board of the Co-ordinating Council of Audiovisual Archives Associations. In 2022, Dennis and Amy helped co-found Missing Movies (www.missingmovies.org), an organization dedicated to assisting filmmakers reclaim their lost rights and locate their original materials. In 2025, Doros returned to his first restoration after forty years, Erich von Stroheim’s Queen Kelly.

items.title: Secret Movie Club
items.description: Be the first to see some of the year’s most exciting new films! On select Tuesday evenings, Secret Movie Club showcases a wide range of titles, from festival favourites and crowd-pleasers to small-budget indies and hidden gems that may not be on everyone’s radar. If you like to attend Galas, Special Presentations, and Centrepiece screenings at the Festival, this series is for you!

guests.bio: Robyn Citizen is the Director of Programming and Platform Lead at the Toronto International Film Festival. Before joining TIFF’s programming team in 2018, she was a film lecturer at the University of British Columbia from 2012 to 2017. In 2010, Citizen spent two months in Japan writing in cat cafés and researching Black American and Japanese encounters in Japanese cinema for part of her dissertation, and a few years later earned a PhD with Distinction in Cinema Studies from New York University. She has published essays in edited volumes and film journals, served on various film festival juries, programmed for the Human Rights Film Festival, and was previously board co-chair of the local Breakthroughs Film Festival.

Keith Bennie is Vice President of Public Programming at the Toronto International Film Festival, overseeing Cinematheque, public programs, community impact, and the Film Reference Library. An arts education leader, he has held roles at the Ontario Science Centre and Theatre Museum Canada, teaches public programming at Humber College, and serves on the Board of Directors at The ArQuives, Canada’s LGBTQ2+ archives.

Peter Kuplowsky is a film curator, producer, and writer based in Toronto. Since 2005, he has championed genre and underground cinema at various international film festivals, including Toronto After Dark, Fantastic Fest (Austin, Texas), and the Toronto International Film Festival, where he currently leads its popular Midnight Madness section. Ever passionate about filmmakers who work outside mainstream channels, Kuplowsky is also the founder and president of Low Sky Productions, an independent film development company. His producer credits include In A Violent Nature, The Void, Psycho Goreman, The Artifice Girl, Climate of the Hunter, Country Gold, Every Heavy Thing, Mangoshake, Camp, and the reboot of Roger Corman's Deathstalker. A graduate of the University of Toronto’s Cinema Studies Institute, Kuplowsky is internationally recognized for his enthusiastic introductions to cult film audiences and his oft-tossed fedora.

items.title: See the North: Standout Shorts by Breakout Directors
items.description: Introduction by TIFF lead programmer for Canada, Jason Anderson, who will be joined by filmmaker Sophy Romvari for a Q&A following the screening.

items.title: Selections by Jeff Wall
items.description: Featuring influential filmmakers Jean-Marie Straub, Danièle Huillet, and Pedro Costa, artist Jeff Wall’s selection of short films foregrounds the process of materialist image-making in lens-based practices. Presented in partnership with Museum of Contemporary Art Toronto (MOCA) as a complement to the exhibition “Jeff Wall Photographs 1984 - 2023”, on view until March 22, 2026. Jeff Wall will be joined by Pedro Costa for a recorded conversation following the screening.

guests.bio:
Born in Lisbon, Pedro Costa left his course of studies in History to attend classes taught by the poet and filmmaker António Reis at the Lisbon Film School. His first film O Sangue (Blood) had its world premiere at the Mostra Cinematografica di Venezia in 1989. Casa de Lava, his second feature, shot in Cabo Verde, screened in Cannes’ Un Certain Regard in 1994. With the following film Ossos, Costa returned to the Venice Film Festival and was awarded an Osella d’Oro in 1997. His other feature films include No quarto da Vanda (In Vanda’s Room), Où gît votre sourire enfoui? (Where does your hidden smile lie?), and many others.

His work has been presented in retrospectives at the main Cinematheques and Film Museums around the world. He has taught at Cal Arts in Los Angeles, the Jan Van Eyck Academy in Maastricht, and at the Film Factory — the film school founded by Béla Tarr — in Sarajevo.

Born in 1946, Jeff Wall has been involved in artistic creation since his childhood in Vancouver. Initially engaged with drawing and painting, Wall took up photography in the late 1960s. After a lengthy period of study and artistic experimentation, Wall began making the photographic art for which he is now renowned in 1977. Wall lives in Vancouver.

Wall has exhibited his photographs internationally for the past forty years including selections in four Documentas, biennales in Venice, São Paulo, Sydney, and Moscow, and solo exhibitions organized by many of the most prestigious art institutions in the world such as Tate, the Museum of Modern Art, the Glenstone Museum, the Art Institute of Chicago, Kunsthalle Mannheim, the Louisiana Museum of Modern Art, and the Bozar Centre for Fine Arts. In 2024–2025, solo presentations of the artist’s work were featured at the Fondation Beyeler, Riehen/Basel, La Virreina Centre de la Imatge, Barcelona, White Cube, London, Gagosian, New York, and the Museum of Art, Architecture, and Technology, Lisbon. In Canada, solo exhibitions have been organized by the Vancouver Art Gallery, the Art Gallery of Ontario, Toronto, the Musée d’art contemporain de Montréal, the Ydessa Hendeles Art Foundation, Toronto, and Canton–sardine, Vancouver.

Wall is acknowledged as one of the most accomplished artist-writers of his generation. His writings range across a variety of topics, from theoretical propositions on photography and art to essays on fellow contemporary artists and major art-historical figures. Wall’s critical writing has been published on many occasions and in several languages. He is an Officer of the Order of Canada and a Fellow of the Royal Society of Canada, and his art is held in numerous public and private collections, including the Vancouver Art Gallery, the Glenstone Museum, Schaulager, the Centre Pompidou, the National Gallery of Canada, Tate, the Museum of Modern Art, and the Art Gallery of Ontario.

Wall has received numerous awards for his photographic works of art, including the Hasselblad Foundation International Award in Photography in 2002, the Roswitha Haftmann-Preis in 2003, and the Audain Prize for the Visual Arts in 2008.

items.title: Sentimental Value
items.imgAlt: Two people in dark clothing stand against a backdrop of green foliage at dusk
items.description: Stellan Skarsgård and Renate Reinsve lead an incomparable cast in Joachim Trier’s moving drama about a director’s bid to revive his career and repair his family’s broken bonds.

items.title: Sirāt
items.imgAlt: Four people on a desert, one wearing a red patterned dress, others in casual attire, against bright sky
items.description:
Presented in Dolby Atmos

A winner of this year’s Cannes Competition Jury Prize, Sirāt is at once a visceral and metaphysical excursion, in which a man desperately searches for his missing daughter amid a roving raver community in the harsh southern deserts of Morocco.

items.title: Sk+te'kmujue'katik (At the Place of Ghosts) with Bretten Hannam
items.imgAlt: Two men stand alert in a misty forest, one carrying supplies in a dark cloth sling
items.description: Post-screening Q&A with Bretten Hannam (director, producer, screenwriter).

guests.imageAlt: At the Place of Ghosts dir Bretten Hannam
guests.bio: Bretten Hannam is a two-spirit L’nu filmmaker living in Kespukwitk, Mi’kma’ki (Nova Scotia) where they were raised. They wrote and directed the shorts New Skin (08), Puppy (10), Deep End (11), and Elmiteskuatl (19), and the features North Mountain (15) and Wildhood (21), which played the Festival. Skɨte’kmujue’katik (At the Place of Ghosts) (25) is their latest film.

items.title: Sound of Falling
items.imgAlt: A young child with blonde braids peeks through a crowd of adults dressed in formal black attire
items.description: Mascha Schilinski’s Cannes Jury Prize–winner braids past and present, telling the history of a tumultuous century through the lives of four girls who spend their respective youths on the same farm in northern Germany.

items.title: Sound of the Mountain
webProgrammes: Daughters, Wives, and Mothers: The Films of Mikio Naruse

items.title: Space Cadet
items.imgAlt: Animated girl and robot sit at table covered in paper origami animals, girl laughing joyfully
items.description: Directed and scored by Kid Koala and based on his graphic novel, Space Cadet is a poignant animated feature with original songs by Karen O and Digable Planets’ Mariana “Ladybug Mecca” Vieira.

items.title: Strengthening the Canadian Screen Sector at TIFF: The Market
items.description:
Discover how TIFF: The Market is designed to elevate Canadian talent and strengthen the Canadian screen sector. This session offers a practical walkthrough of the Market’s structure, programming, and international reach, highlighting how it supports both creative development and business-to-business activity across film, series, and innovation. Attendees will gain insight into how the Market connects creators and companies with buyers, partners, and industry leaders, and how its programming advances projects and supports international market readiness.

This session features a moderated conversation with members of the TIFF: The Market Advisory Committee, including Noah Segal (Elevation Pictures), Niv Fichman (Rhombus Media), and Laura Michalchyshyn (Blue Ant Media). It will offer guidance for Canadian creators and companies on navigating the Market with confidence, from shaping strategic goals and refining pitches to building meaningful industry relationships, securing partnerships, and positioning work for success in global markets.

guests.title: Moderator
guests.bio: Vice President, Market Programming & Theatrical

Noah Segal is co-president and co-founder of Elevation Pictures. Since its launch in 2014, Elevation has established itself as a leading independent distributor in Canada with Academy Award–winning titles such as The Imitation Game, Moonlight, Room, and The Father alongside box office hits Lady Bird, Paw Patrol: The Movie, Hustlers, Everything Everywhere All at Once, Civil War, and Longlegs. In 2016, Segal expanded his duties to spearhead Elevation’s new production division with producer Christina Piovesan. Elevation has been actively producing and co-producing original film and TV content in Canada. Projects include French Exit directed by Azazel Jacobs, starring Michelle Pfeiffer and Lucas Hedges, and The Nest directed by Sean Durkin, starring Jude Law and Carrie Coon. Recent projects include Alice, Darling directed by Mary Nighy and starring Anna Kendrick, A Good Person directed by Zack Braff and starring Florence Pugh and Morgan Freeman, and Infinity Pool directed by Brandon Cronenberg and starring Alexander Skarsgård. Currently, the company is delivering the acclaimed documentaries Disco’s Revenge and Lilith Fair.

guests.imageAlt: A person with silver hair and glasses smiling warmly in a black and white photo
guests.bio: Niv Fichman is a veteran independent film and television producer and founder of Rhombus Media, specializing in theatrical feature films, high-end television drama, and performing arts programmes. Mr. Fichman has produced feature films by renowned international directors like Denis Villeneuve, Olivier Assayas, Atom Egoyan, Francois Girard, Guy Maddin, & Brandon Cronenberg. Notable past films include The Red Violin, Clean, Thirty Two Short Films about Glenn Gould, and most recently Seven Viels, Blackberry, and the 7-part series The Sympathizer. Rhombus & Fichman’s dedication to excellence is reflected in the awards the company’s projects have received, including an Oscar, seven Emmys, and a Golden Rose of Montreux. In 2007 Mr. Fichman was awarded the Canadian Producer of the Year by the CFTPA and was honored by the Canadian Civil Liberties Association in 2011.

Laura Michalchyshyn is an accomplished producer and media executive with over 30 years in film and television. She recently produced several notable documentary features and series, including Paul Anka: His Way, Epstein’s Shadow: Ghislaine Maxwell (Peacock) Jerry Brown: The Disrupter (PBS), Kids in the Hall: Comedy Punks (Prime Video), and John Lewis: Good Trouble (CNN/Magnolia). Most recently, Laura served as Chief Creative Officer and Co-President of Global Production at Blue Ant Media co-managing growth, content creation and development across its various production companies in North America and Asia-Pacific. She continues to executive produce the successful Canada’s Drag Race franchises with Blue Ant, Bell Media and World of Wonder and consults with Blue Ant Studios.

Previously, Laura co-founded Sundance Productions with Robert Redford, producing Momentum Generation (HBO), The American West (AMC), and Death Row Stories (CNN). She has partnered with major talent and production companies, including Dawn Porter, Alex Gibney, Marina Zenovich, Joe Berlinger, Isabella Rossellini, Guy Maddin, Lisa Cortes, Jeff and Michael Zimbalist, World of Wonder and Radical Media.

Laura has also led programming and development teams in the U.S. and Canada at Sundance Channel, Planet Green (Discovery) and Showcase (Alliance Atlantis). Her accolades include three News and Documentary Emmys, a Sports Emmy, several Canadian Screen Awards, and a Peabody Award. In 2024, she was named to The Hollywood Reporter’s first- ever list of Canada’s most powerful women in entertainment. Laura works and resides in New York City.

guests.title: Panelist
guests.bio: Head of Market

items.title: Sudden Rain with Kelley Dong
items.description: Introduction by writer, producer, and filmmaker Kelley Dong
webProgrammes: Daughters, Wives, and Mothers: The Films of Mikio Naruse

guests.bio: Kelley Dong is a writer, producer, and filmmaker. Their writing has appeared in MUBI Notebook, Cinema Scope, Film Comment, The Village Voice, and the Harvard Film Archive.

items.title: TIFF Advance Screening: 3670
items.imgAlt: Group gathered in a dimly lit club space with pink neon lighting and "Dirty Heaven" posters on the wall
items.description: Join us for the Toronto Premiere of the acclaimed Korean independent film 3670, winner of four awards at the renowned Jeonju International Film Festival. Presented in partnership with HanVoice, an international charity in Canada working to improve human rights in North Korea, and Rainbow Railroad, a global not-for-profit organization that helps at-risk LGBTQI+ people get to safety worldwide, the screening will be followed by a live Zoom Q&A with writer-director Joonho Park about his tender and luminous portrayal of North Korean defector communities and LGBTQ+ communities in contemporary Korea.

guests.bio: Sean Chung serves as the CEO of HanVoice, an international charity based in Canada dedicated to improving human rights in North Korea. He successfully led advocacy efforts in Canada to create a historic public policy that allows North Korean refugees to access Canada’s Private Sponsorship of Refugees pathway in 2021. Chung has organized international and regional conferences on human rights, peace, and security in the Korean Peninsula. Before his work at HanVoice, he was a consultant at the International Organization for Migration and an officer at the United Nations Major Group for Children and Youth in New York. He earned his BA in Political Science from the University of Toronto.

Joonho Park studied film directing at the Korean National University of Arts, where he made the short films EUNSEO (2019) and Almond: My Voice is Breaking(2017). He worked as a screenplay writer before making his first feature debut 3670 (2025).

items.title: The Discreet Charm of the Bourgeoisie
The Misconceived with James N. Kienitz Wilkins
items.description: Introduction by MDFF Selects co-founder and curator Kazik Radwanski who will be joined by filmmaker James N. Kienitz Wilkins and actor Jesse Wakeman following the screening.

guests.bio: James N. Kienitz Wilkins (director/co-writer) is a filmmaker and writer. Features include Still Film (MoMA Doc Fortnight ‘23), The Plagiarists (Berlinale ‘19), Common Carrier (BAMcinemaFest ‘17), and Public Hearing (‘12). In 2017, he was included in the Whitney Biennial. His writing has appeared in Triple Canopy, The Metrograph, Filmmaker Magazine, the Brooklyn Rail, and MUBI Notebook, among other publications. automaticmoving.substack.com

Jesse Wakeman (performer “Tobin”) is a New York-based filmmaker and actor whose work has played at SXSW, New Directors/New Films, Rooftop Film Festival, AFI, and the Locarno International Film Festival. He is the co-creator, writer and star of the Roughhouse-produced 2016 feature, Donald Cried. He was nominated for an Independent Spirit Award in 2016. Wakeman was the Director of Photography on the 2021 award-winning feature documentary, Who We Are: A Chronicle of Racism in America and is currently in production with the same team on the documentary series, Another Country. He is the co-writer of the award winning narrative feature Bruce!!! He has shown films at The Fridman Gallery, MOCADA, The Invisible Dog, Anthology Film Archives, The Neiman Gallery, and The Fischer Landau Center for Art. He holds an MFA in Visual Arts from Columbia University.

Paul Dallas (co-producer and voice of Kirby) is a Canadian American writer and filmmaker based in Brooklyn. He co-wrote the HBO docu-drama Reality, which won a 2023 Peabody Award. Producing credits include the documentaries Halston (CNN Films/Amazon) and the 2024 NAACP Image Award winner Invisible Beauty (Magnolia Pictures), both of which premiered at Sundance. He also produced the acclaimed indie comedy The Plagiarists, a New York Times Critics’ Pick. His new documentary short Division will premiere at True/False 2026 and has been selected for New Directors/New Films 2026. His writing on film has appeared in Artforum, BOMB, Extra Extra Magazine, Filmmaker Magazine, IndieWire, the Village Voice, and elsewhere. He is a 2025 MacDowell Fellow.

items.title: The Red Spectacles with Zachary Goldkind
items.imgAlt: Dark figure wearing plague doctor mask with glowing red eyes in a shadowy environment
items.description: Introduction by filmmaker, programmer, and scholar Zachary Goldkind.

guests.bio: Zachary Goldkind is an experimental/narrative filmmaker, festival programmer, and cultural scholar based in Tkaronto/Toronto, so-called Canada. Goldkind holds an MFA in Film Production from York University. Their studies orbit subjects of image-ideology and narrative theory, taking careful consideration of the role aesthetics play in producing and reproducing cultural hegemonies. Their work in all sectors is guided by a quote by Paulo Freire, the Brazilian educator and Marxist philosopher whose important pedagogical work was undermined by the American-backed coup in 1964, which led to the two-decade-long military dictatorship and Freire’s exile. He says, “Radicalization, nourished by a critical spirit, is always creative.”

items.title: The Secret Agent
items.imgAlt: Man in white shirt using vintage red payphone against wall covered in black and white political posters
items.description: Winner of multiple prizes at this year’s Cannes Film Festival, TIFF veteran Kleber Mendonça Filho’s sly, genre-bending political thriller stars Wagner Moura as Marcelo, a technology expert on the lam and seeking refuge in the Brazilian city of Recife in 1977.

items.title: The Sky Trembles and the Earth is Afraid and the Two Eyes Are Not Brothers
The Stranger within a Woman
webProgrammes: Daughters, Wives, and Mothers: The Films of Mikio Naruse

items.title: The Things You Kill with Alireza Khatami
items.imgAlt: Two people smoking at night on a rooftop with city lights twinkling in the dark background
items.description: Post-screening Q&A with Alireza Khatami (director).

guests.bio: Alireza Khatami is an award-winning American Iranian writer, director, and producer based in Canada. Born into the Indigenous Khamse tribe in southeast Iran, he was deeply influenced by the rich oral storytelling traditions of his heritage. This inspiration led him to create a unique storytelling style, blending fantasy and realistic narratives. His works poignantly explore precarious lives, identity politics, and feature a dark sense of humor. Starting as an assistant director, Alireza worked with esteemed filmmakers, including Asghar Farhadi. His films gained recognition, showcased at prestigious festivals such as Cannes, Venice, and Berlinale. His debut feature, Oblivion Verses, premiered at the 74th Venice Film Festival, winning three awards, including the Orizzonti Award for Best Screenplay and FIPRESCI. His second feature, Terrestrial Verses, premiered in the Un Certain Regard section at Cannes. Currently, Khatami is an associate professor at Toronto Metropolitan University and working on his third feature film, Things That You Kill, a psychological thriller critiquing patriarchy.

items.title: The Voice of Hind Rajab
items.imgAlt: Three people gathered around looking at a man seated at a table with his laptop speaking into a mobile phone
items.description: Oscar-nominated director Kaouther Ben Hania blends actual recordings and scripted performances to tell the devastating true story of Hind Rajab, a Palestinian girl trapped in a car under Israeli military fire, and the first responders who tried to save her.

items.title: The Whole Family Works preceded by Hideko, the Bus Conductor
webProgrammes: Daughters, Wives, and Mothers: The Films of Mikio Naruse

items.title: Train Dreams
items.imgAlt: A person stands on wooden railroad tracks winding through a dark evergreen forest at dusk
items.description:
Screening in 35mm!

Set in the Pacific Northwest during the first decades of the 20th century, Jockey director Clint Bentley’s beguiling adaptation of Pulitzer Prize finalist Denis Johnson’s novella stars Joel Edgerton as a humble labourer immersed in a rapidly changing world of natural splendour and voracious industry.

items.title: Tuner
items.imgAlt: Two men standing on a porch with stone columns, one wearing headphones and one in a brown jacket holding a drink
items.description: Documentarian Daniel Roher dazzles with his narrative debut, a whip-smart thriller about an unusual safecracker (Leo Woodall). Co-starring Dustin Hoffman and Havana Rose Liu.

items.title: Uiksaringitara (Wrong Husband) with Zacharias Kunuk
items.imgAlt: Two people in winter coats share a tender moment in warm lighting against a dark background
items.description: Post-screening Q&A with Zacharias Kunuk (director, screenwriter, executive producer).

items.title: Under the Hanging Tree with Perivi Katjavivi and Kivu Ruhorahoza
items.description:
Introduction by Associate Professor of Historical Studies at University of Toronto Mississauga, Julie MacArthur, who will be joined by filmmakers Perivi Katjavivi and Kivu Ruhorahoza for a Q&A following the screening.

Due to unforeseen circumstances, director Kivu Ruhorahoza will no longer be in attendance for this screening.
webProgrammes: What Remains: Reclaiming Memories, Materialities, and Embodied Histories in African Cinema

guests.bio:
Perivi John Katjavivi is a Namibian filmmaker whose work has garnered international acclaim for its innovative exploration of African history, identity, and storytelling. His feature films have premiered at major festivals such as the Berlinale, Rotterdam, and Busan, and in 2023 he made history with Under the Hanging Tree — Namibia’s first-ever submission to the Academy Awards® in the Best International Feature Film category. This film, which blends elements of noir and mysticism, also won two major awards at the Africa Movie Academy Awards (AMAA) last year, including Best Editing and the Michael Anyiam Osigwe Award for Best Film by an African Living Abroad.

Katjavivi holds a BA in Cinema from Columbia College Hollywood and an MA in African Cinema from the University of Cape Town, where his research focused on how Namibian film can reshape post-colonial narratives. He is currently a PhD candidate in History at the University of the Western Cape. In addition to Under the Hanging Tree, his filmography includes Emoyeni: Nsanguluko (2018), which was on Netflix and won 3 SAFTAs (South African Film and Television Awards).

Outside of directing and producing, Katjavivi is the founder of the Return to the Source Film Lab — a Pan-African workshop and residency program that merges film education with the investigation of sites tied to colonial history and trauma. Funded by the Mastercard Enablement Fund through the Berlinale, the Goethe Institute, and the Namibia Film Commission, it blends oral history, archival research, and practical filmmaking. Perivi is also a licensed Anglican lay minister, serving at St George’s Cathedral in Windhoek, Namibia, reflecting his deep commitment to spiritual life and community service alongside his filmmaking career. Perivi serves as a mentor for emerging filmmakers through Durban FilmMart's Future Mentors program and was selected to pitch his upcoming project The Man with the Crooked Arm at the 2024 Berlinale Co-Production Market, as reported in Variety.

guests.bio: Greg de Cuir Jr is the co-founder and artistic director of Kinopravda Institute in Belgrade. He has organized programmes for Locarno Film Festival, Anthology Film Archives, Austrian Filmmuseum, Metrograph, Eye Filmmuseum, Media City Film Festival, and many other institutions. His writing has been published in Cineaste, Millennium Film Journal, Jump Cut, Found Footage Magazine, and numerous anthologies and catalogues. De Cuir was previously selector and organizer at Alternative Film Video in Belgrade.

Daughters, Wives, and Mothers: The Films.title: Daughters, Wives, and Mothers: The Films of Mikio Naruse
Daughters, Wives, and Mothers: The Films.pitch:
Emerging as one of Japan’s most perceptive chroniclers of everyday life, Mikio Naruse was born 120 years ago on August 20, 1905. TIFF Cinematheque is proud to present this 19-film retrospective Daughters, Wives and Mothers: the Films of Mikio Naruse — the first since Cinematheque Ontario’s retrospective in 2006. Curated around a North American tour of six 35mm prints from the Japan Foundation (Repast, When A Woman Ascends the Stairs, Yearning, Late Chrysanthemums, Floating Clouds, and Scattered Clouds), Daughters, Wives and Mothers offers an opportunity to rediscover Naruse’s legacy on the big screen.

Naruse’s compassionate focus on ordinary, working-class people stemmed from his experience growing up in poverty. His childhood was marked by financial instability and precarious family ties, circumstances common to the film subgenre he helped define: shoshimin-geki (common people drama). “If they move even a little,” Naruse famously remarked of his characters, “they quickly hit the wall. From the youngest age, I have thought that the world we live in betrays us; this thought still remains with me.”

Unable to pursue his dream of being a novelist, Naruse dropped out of school and became a prop assistant at Shochiku Studios. Directors Yoshinobu Ikeda and Heinosuke Gosho mentored Naruse as he directed his first short films, including Flunky, Work Hard! (1931), and several feature-length silent melodramas. His early work was marked by playful transitions, expressive cinematography, and comedic touches. Flunky, Work Hard! displayed themes Naruse would revisit throughout his career, including strained marriages, emotionally distant husbands with minimal ambition, and wives burdened by resentment and overwork.

After languishing for a decade within Shochiku’s roster and being dismissively referred to as a “second Ozu”, Naruse moved to P.C.L. and achieved his first major breakthrough with Wife! Be Like a Rose (1935), a critical and commercial success later declared the “Best Film of the Year” by Kinema Junpo. It also became one of the first Japanese films to receive a theatrical release in the United States.

Naruse continued to streamline his style, uniquely contributing to the shoshimin-geki subgenre through deceptively fluid editing that masked multiple cuts on movement, a focus on characterization through subtle gestures, and a sensitivity to spatial continuity. Critic Shigehiko Hasumi observed that Naruse had “mastered the special grasp of space that directors who debuted during the silent era acquired, and internalized this skill to transform limitations into a positive force. His adherence to natural space is a distinguishing feature of Naruse’s work.” In The Whole Family Works (1939), this interplay of spaces illuminated family tensions through cramped living spaces, the search for privacy in cafés and bars, and the intrusive sounds of home, reflecting the parents’ economic anxieties and the children’s symbolic rebellion.

Naruse later began his defining collaboration with Hideko Takamine on Hideko, the Bus Conductor (1941). Having recently transitioned from child star to adult actor, Takamine went on to appear in 17 of Naruse’s films, portraying strong-willed, self-possessed women. Her performances in Floating Clouds (1955) and Yearning (1964) — two of Naruse’s starkest masterpieces of longing and disappointment — and her complex incarnation of an aging bar hostess in the evocatively titled When A Woman Ascends the Stairs (1960) illustrated how Naruse and Takamine deepened each other’s art and how she translated his quietly despairing realism into lived emotion.

Repast (1951) is often regarded as the beginning of Naruse’s late-career resurgence following several commercially routine studio assignments. It also marked the start of his profound engagement with Fumiko Hayashi’s writings, whose frank portrayals of working-class women resonated deeply with Naruse and his ability to render female subjectivities with psychological depth on screen. A quote attributed to Hayashi in Repast’s credits says, “How could I love the pitiful human lives more than I do, the pitiful human lives, which survive in this infinitely wide Universe?” Accordingly, Hayashi compassionately depicted her heroines seeking freedom amid constrained urban life and disappointing romances.

Other highlights of the series include Late Chrysanthemums (1954), Sudden Rain (1956), and Flowing (1956), which represent what is recognized as the golden age of Naruse’s career, refining his exploration of gendered labour, personal agency, and the everyday pressures shaping women’s lives.

Naruse’s legacy extends far beyond the oft-repeated title of the “fourth master” of Japanese cinema, which situated him alongside Akira Kurosawa, Yasujiro Ozu, and Kenji Mizoguchi, whose films initially received greater international exposure and critical attention. During his career spanning more than 30 years, Mikio Naruse translated the contradictions of Japanese modernity into an enduring form of popular cinema through his deft use of melodramatic themes, realist cinematography, and sustained attention to women.

While Naruse was never overtly political, in the contemporary landscape of deepening inequality and constant promotion of aspirational lifestyles, it feels timely to celebrate a filmmaker who was singularly committed to depicting the struggles of ordinary people with emotional depth and dignity.

ROBYN CITIZEN

Daughters, Wives, and Mothers: The Films of Mikio Naruse series is generously supported by the Japan Foundation, Toronto.

From the Collection.title: From the Collection
From the Collection.pitch:
We enter films from anywhere. It could be the story that sweeps us up. It could be watching a character who becomes more compelling with each scene. It could be the details ― the way a shot is framed, the way light hits a costume, or the way a music cue drops at just the right moment. Or, it could be all of this, plus the visceral pleasure of watching light dance on the big screen.

For me, that experience hits a new level when we watch a 35mm print of a great film. That’s how I began watching films in the pre-digital era, and that’s what I want to bring to life as often as possible in our cinemas.

TIFF is fortunate enough to be the caretakers of 3,500 35mm film prints, held at a storage facility down the highway from TIFF Bell Lightbox. We began this work in 1990, when we acquired the collection of the Ontario Film Institute. In 2016, as film distributors completed their transition from 35mm prints to digital cinema packages (DCPs), we acquired print collections from Universal Pictures, Mongrel Media, eOne, and others. Our promise then was to protect and preserve film on film.

Partly, it’s practical. Film prints remain the most durable, stable way to preserve what filmmakers create. Under the right conditions, they can last a century.

Partly, it’s curatorial. When a film is shot on 35mm, screening it in the same format preserves the intentions of the artist as closely as possible.

And partly, it’s emotional. Projecting light through the physical strip of film, at 24 still frames every second, offers a richer, warmer experience than even the purest digital copy. It’s like listening to a favourite album on vinyl, or picking up a physical book and savouring the turning of each page, instead of swiping a screen.

Analogue. There’s nothing like it.

But it can’t be just any film print. For this new series, From the Collection, I went through our list of films carefully, almost obsessively. Which films show us something central to film history? Which films are so cinematic that they cry out to be seen on the big screen? Or so rare that they can’t be found on a streaming service? Or so provocative that they’re bound to start conversations, bound to send you home more alive to the possibilities of art in your world? Which films are simply so great that you want to share them with people you care about?

Today, we live in an infinite flood of digital images. From the Collection is our invitation to step in the other direction.

One film each month (except September, when we always have something special for you).

Chosen with care from our 35mm collection.

Presented by some of the best cinema technicians in the world. In Canada’s best cinema.

With conversation afterwards in Varda, our newly renovated third-floor lounge bar.

Join us on the first Tuesday of each month for a truly unique film experience.

CAMERON BAILEY

Luis Bu\u00f1uel: Desire and Deviance.title: Luis Buñuel: Desire and Deviance
Luis Bu\u00f1uel: Desire and Deviance.pitch:
“Thus Buñuel stands out as one of the great names of the cinema at the end of the silent screen and the beginning of sound — one with which only that of Vigo bears comparison.” (André Bazin, The Cinema of Cruelty)

“Can there be any spectacle more terrible than the sight of a cloud obscuring the moon at its full? The prologue can hardly have one indifferent. It tells us that in this film we must see with a different eye.” —Jean Vigo on Un chien andalou

Through a body of work spanning 35 films and a half-century, the legacy of Luis Buñuel (1900–1983) as one of cinema’s greatest poets and unrepentant iconoclasts has lost none of its potency or significance in the subsequent forty years after his death. Born at the turn of the 19th century into a Catholic family in the Aragon region of Spain, Buñuel’s distaste for the institutionalized evils of the world was first catalyzed in a strict Jesuit education and later refined upon his first encounter with the avant-garde in France (the country which Buñuel would begin and end his career in), where his entré into André Breton’s Surrealist group was borne from two collaborations with painter Salvador Dalí — Un chien andalou (1929) and L'Âge d'or (1930). Rightly heralded as the zenith of Surrealist cinema, Buñuel’s auspicious debut prefigures the same totemic fixations that he would continue to mine throughout his career, notably a corrosive view of religious dogma, bourgeois hypocrisies, and all manner of institutions held sacred, all imagined through a confrontation between the irrational and perverse. The associative heresies Buñuel conjures between desire and deviance or sacred and profane reimagine the human condition as something beyond good and evil and altogether more mysterious, deserving of our understanding and empathy.

Perhaps one of cinema’s more singular examples of a transnational filmmaker, Buñuel would leave Europe after witnessing the rise of fascism for Hollywood, in which he would spend a brief and misunderstood interlude, and depart once again for Mexico — the country where he would become a citizen and call home for the remainder of his life. Buñuel’s Mexico period stands as one of the auteur’s most fertile and transcendent, yet also stunted — not making a film for over a decade. The breakthrough success of Los Olvidados in 1950 would bring the filmmaker international recognition once more. Later inciting a scandal through the Palme d’Or–winning Viridiana (1961), where he would be once again censored by Spain’s Franco regime and condemned by the Vatican itself, Buñuel would embark on a transformative final chapter in France, where he would complete a string of career-capping masterworks.

Bridging his incendiary and unforgettable debut (Un chien andalou, 1929) with his explosive final film (That Obscure Object of Desire, 1977), this retrospective assembles a selection of Luis Buñuel’s greatest scandals in a set of recent restorations, beginning with Viridiana (1961) premiering in a new and breathtaking 4K restoration, and expanded upon with rarities on 35 mm, including his criminally underseen and rarely screened adaptation of Wuthering Heights (1954). The strength and integrity of Buñuel’s output is evidenced by the number of regular and steadfast collaborators, such icons of the screen as Silvia Pinal, Fernando Rey, Michel Piccoli, and screenwriter Jean-Claude Carrière, but the legacy of his searing vision is most eloquently articulated by Andrei Tarkovsky, who called Buñuel “the bearer, above all else, of poetic consciousness.”

“It was a worldview fraught with contradictions, defined by patience and scorn, peopled with pious sinners and debauched saints, visually Spartan and yet spasming with bouts of the irrational. This was Luis’s world — welcome to it.” —Michael Atkinson, Criterion Current

AMANDA BRASON

MDFF Selects.title: MDFF Selects
MDFF Selects.pitch: Our showcase of provocative and challenging new cinema from around the world.

Midnight Madness Presents.title: Midnight Madness Presents
Midnight Madness Presents.pitch: Since 1988, TIFF’s annual Midnight Madness programme has presented the wildest and strangest cinematic provocations from around the world while cultivating an infectiously raucous audience experience. The madness now extends to a monthly series at TIFF Lightbox — commemorating past selections and inducting older midnight classics into our ever-evolving canon.

Oliver Laxe: Modern Mystic.title: Oliver Laxe: Modern Mystic
Oliver Laxe: Modern Mystic.pitch:
“In my films, I want to show a concept that is dear to me: that of a sovereign submission that we can define as such: accepting that we are not free renders us free.” —Oliver Laxe

Born in Paris in 1982 to Galician parents, Oliver Laxe has shaped one of the most distinctive spiritual cinemas of the past decade and a half. With four features in 15 years — all having premiered at Cannes, all awarded — his filmmaking has remained radically unhurried, displaying a desire for transcendence. His cinema may be rife with imposing dilemmas and circumstances, portraying physical and mental tribulations, yet it is ultimately one that receives and surrenders. His use of non-professional actors, real locations, and analogue film, as well as his willingness to submit to chance during shooting, have become hallmarks of his work.

After studying film in Barcelona, Laxe spent 12 formative years in Tangier running a filmmaking workshop for youth in foster care. That experience developed into You Are All Captains (2010), a genre-defying debut that moves freely between documentary, fiction, and metafiction. Laxe plays a sardonic version of himself — a self-absorbed European director whose good intentions are wrapped in colonialist presumption. But the film, with great playfulness and grace, slowly dissolves this conceit. As the children reclaim the camera, the narrative sheds its false skin and opens outward and onto a sun-soaked pastoral journey guided not by the director but by the rambunctious young rebels who have freed themselves from his control. Shot on black-and-white 35mm, You Are All Captains garnered critical comparisons to Abbas Kiarostami and Pier Paolo Pasolini, and announced Laxe’s abiding interests in dignity, observation, and the redemptive potential of play. It also introduced Shakib Ben Omar, the film’s magnetic conscience and a recurring figure in Laxe’s universe.

Mimosas (2016) extended this sensibility into the realm of the mythical. Set amid Morocco’s majestic Atlas Mountains, the film follows a caravan’s attempt to carry a dying sheikh to his ancestral burial grounds. When he dies en route, it falls to a wild-eyed mystic, played again by Shakib, to guide the remaining travellers through perilous terrain. Shot on 16mm by Mauro Herce — one of Laxe’s closest collaborators — Mimosas feels simultaneously ancient and startlingly modern, invoking Sufi mysticism, westerns, and wandering parables. Its spiritual stakes are embedded in the landscapes themselves, which loom over the characters and subsume their fears. Laxe’s belief that cinema must “accept what life brings” is evident everywhere: storms roll in, horses balk, paths vanish, taxis appear out of nowhere, and the film bends to these contingencies with a quiet, radical faith.

That faith returns in a more crepuscular register in Fire Will Come (2019), Laxe’s first film made on Galician soil. Set in the mountains and eucalyptus forests of Ancares, the film follows Amador, a man recently released from prison for arson, and his elderly mother, Benedicta. Here, Laxe observes, with ethnographic tenderness, the gestures of villagers and their isolation, the rhythms of rural labour, and the relationship between humans and a land growing increasingly volatile. Fire becomes both destroyer and purifier, a paradox mirrored in Laxe’s interest in forgiveness and fate. Benedicta’s simple line, “If they impose suffering, it is because they suffer,” could stand as an epigraph for his entire body of work.

In Sirāt, his latest and most powerful film yet, Laxe returns to Morocco but penetrates new territory: more contemporary, more urgent, yet spiritually contiguous with his earlier films. The title, which means “path” in Arabic, invokes the Sirāt Bridge of Islamic eschatology, the narrow sliver between damnation and salvation. The metaphysical thriller follows Luis (Sergi López) and his young son Esteban (Bruno Núñez) as they arrive at a mountain rave, distributing flyers for Mar — their missing daughter and sister. Rumours of her presence propel them deeper into the desert, where they join a caravan of motley ravers and wanderers, all memorably played by non-professional actors.

Shot again on textured 16mm by Herce, Sirāt oscillates between the intimate and the cosmic. Its images — flares of light on sand, silhouettes dissolving into darkness, glimpses of soldiers — suggest a world on the cusp of upheaval. Working with a musician for the first time, Laxe enlisted electronic composer and DJ Kangding Ray to create a score; the resulting low-end heavy techno increasingly transforms the gruelling expedition into a sensorial and hypnotic experience that tests physical and psychological limits.

If Mimosas was a pilgrimage and Fire Will Come an elegy, Sirāt is a reckoning and a reset. Hints of political collapse, displacement, and ecological catastrophe bleed into the narrative, yet Laxe resists didacticism. Instead, he stages startling encounters between anguish and release; between human fragility and the indifference of the desert; between dancing and death. The film culminates in a vision both mysterious and prophetic, further proof that in Laxe’s cinema, transcendence lies not in escape but in acceptance. In a turbulent world, his films stand as rare offerings — ascetic yet generous, intimate yet otherworldly — reminding us that freedom sometimes begins by relinquishing or losing control.

ANDRÉA PICARD

See The North.title: See The North
See The North.pitch: In 2025, See The North — TIFF’s monthly, free series of Canadian cinematic treasures — celebrates the work of Canadian Women filmmakers programmed in the years since our very first Festival.

TIFF Cinematheque New and Restored.title: TIFF Cinematheque New and Restored
TIFF Cinematheque New and Restored.pitch: A selection of recent restorations that have been painstakingly brought back to life in revived cinematic presentations.

TIFF Cinematheque Special Screenings.title: TIFF Cinematheque Special Screenings
TIFF Cinematheque Special Screenings.pitch: TIFF Cinematheque’s ongoing series of classics, favourites, rarities, and recent restorations.

TIFF Lightbox.pitch: Tours, workshops, and activities for all ages.

TIFF Next Wave Presents.title: TIFF Next Wave Presents

What Remains: Reclaiming Memories, Mater.title: What Remains: Reclaiming Memories, Materialities, and Embodied Histories in African Cinema
What Remains: Reclaiming Memories, Mater.pitch:
When does the past become history? When experiences of colonization, struggle, and violence erase, bury, or displace the memories, materialities, and the very bodies of the past, what remains?

The What Remains film series is an exciting co-presentation by TIFF Cinematheque and the Cinema Studies Institute at the University of Toronto, co-curated by Dr. Julie MacArthur and Dr. Alison MacAulay. This series highlights an emerging trend in African cinema, exploring how filmmakers on the continent are using film and visual media to confront these questions and reclaim history in visually striking and deeply personal ways.

This series examines how African filmmakers of diverse backgrounds, often in collaboration with scholars and researchers, use film to reconstitute what remains of the past. Whether through fiction or documentary, these films work to recover missing and fragmented remnants — in archives, material objects, personal memories, and physical bodies — to reconcile difficult and often violent histories. While spotlighting films from across the African continent, this series grapples with urgent global questions around history and reconciliation, archival ownership and access, and legacies of settler colonialism and violence. The result is a rich tapestry of unique perspectives that unearth hidden memories, lost archives, and untold stories that challenge viewers to reconsider the ongoing traumas of historical loss, reclaim the present, and imagine new futures.

Join us for this exciting and diverse line-up of African documentary and fiction films that span time, space, and genre: from fast-paced investigative documentaries and surrealist docufictions to meditative experimental shorts and gripping crime thrillers. Experience the breadth and vibrancy of African cinema, featuring films from the Democratic Republic of Congo, Guinea-Bissau, Kenya, Namibia, and Rwanda. Select screenings will include special guests and post-screening Q&As!

The What Remains series runs January 9–16, 2026, with screenings at TIFF Lightbox and Innis Town Hall Theatre. For the full programme and venues, see https://sites.utm.utoronto.ca/whatremainsfilmseries/

JULIE MACARTHUR AND ALISON MACAULAY
